blurb: A Plant Select selection of the Southwest's golden columbine: big, fragrant, clear-yellow flowers with long elegant spurs that nod and sway from late spring to frost — larger, longer-blooming, and far more heat- and drought-tolerant than the blue.
water: Moderate; tolerates dry shade once established
spread: Clumping & short-lived; reseeds generously to persist
seasons: Golden long-spurred flowers late spring–frost; ferny blue-green foliage; cut back to a fresh rosette in midsummer heat
wildlife: Hummingbird, bee & butterfly nectar; long bloom feeds them for months
toxic: Flowers were historically nibbled in tiny amounts for their sweet nectar, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are poisonous if eaten in quantity — not a safe food
origin: Southwestern US mountains (AZ, NM, S Colorado, W Texas)
habitat: moist canyon seeps, streamsides & rocky limestone slopes

care.hardiness: Hardy across USDA zones 3–8 — fully winter-hardy on the Front Range (Boulder is ~5b–6a). The foliage dies back after hard frost and re-emerges in early spring. It is one of the more heat- and drought-tolerant columbines, native to hot Southwestern canyons, so it shrugs off Front Range summers better than the blue columbine.[1][2]
care.sun: Sun to part shade — one of its virtues is its wide adaptability to light. Full sun is fine here with adequate moisture, but at hot, dry sites afternoon shade keeps the foliage from scorching and fading; it also tolerates and blooms in dry shade better than most columbines. The hotter and drier the spot, the more afternoon shade it appreciates.[1][2]
care.soil: Average to rich, well-drained soil; in the wild it grows in moist, gritty canyon and limestone-seep soils, so it takes our alkaline ground in stride as long as drainage is good. Amend heavy clay with compost and grit. Mulch the root zone to keep it cool and hold moisture.[1][2]
care.water: Moderate; it likes more consistent moisture than the xeric natives but is notably drought-tolerant for a columbine once established. Water deeply through bloom and summer heat, easing off as it goes dormant. Avoid wetting the foliage, which invites mildew.[1][2]
care.spacing: Space about 15–20 in. apart; mature plants reach roughly 2–3 ft tall and 1.5 ft wide. Individual plants are short-lived (3–5 years) but self-seed generously to persist.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant nursery-grown starts in spring (April–May) or early fall (September) — both windows work on the Front Range. Spring planting establishes plants through the season; early-fall planting gives roots time to settle before freeze-up. It grows happily in a 10–12 in. container, but potted roots are far more cold-exposed than in-ground ones: in Boulder winters sink the pot to its rim in a sheltered spot, wrap it in burlap and mulch, or move it to an unheated garage by late October. An in-ground plant in the same zone needs no protection.[1][2]
care.propagation: Seed is the easiest and most reliable method and how it perpetuates itself in the garden (see sow/stratify/depth). 'Denver Gold' is a seed-grown selection and comes fairly true from seed, though garden columbines cross-pollinate freely, so plant it away from other columbines if you want clean color. Division is possible but not ideal — columbines develop a taproot and resent disturbance, so divided plants often sulk; if you must, do it in very early spring or early fall, keeping as much root as possible. The most sustainable strategy is to let it self-seed and replace aging crowns naturally.[1][2]
care.sow: Direct-sow in early fall for natural cold-stratification over winter, or cold-stratify in the refrigerator for about 30 days before a spring sow. Surface-sow or barely cover — the seed needs light to germinate. Plants bloom in their second year from seed.[2]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (about 3–4 weeks in damp medium in the refrigerator) before a spring sow to break dormancy; fall-sown seed stratifies naturally outdoors. Without chilling, spring-sown seed germinates poorly and unevenly.[2]
care.depth: Sow on the surface and press in, or cover with no more than ⅛–¼ in. of fine soil — light aids germination. Soil around 60–70°F promotes germination in a few weeks; seedlings emerge slowly, so be patient.[2]
care.bloom: Late spring to frost — the big, fragrant, long-spurred golden flowers (often over 3 in. across) open in May and, if deadheaded, rebloom into autumn, a far longer season than the blue columbine. Remove spent flowers in late June to push the longest possible bloom.[1][2]
care.feeding: A light spring top-dressing of compost is plenty. Avoid high-nitrogen feeds, which produce leafy growth at the expense of flowers.[1]
care.maintenance: Deadhead spent flowers to prolong bloom and tidy the plant; leave a few to self-seed. When the foliage tires and yellows in midsummer heat, cut the whole plant back to the ground — it flushes a fresh rosette of clean leaves for fall. Watch for and snip out leafminer-tracked leaves.[1][2]
care.selfsow: Self-seeds generously — volunteers appear near parent plants the following spring, which is how this short-lived perennial persists. Seed crosses readily with other columbines, so isolate it to keep the golden color true.[1][2]
care.troubles: Columbine leafminer tunnels pale squiggly tracks through the leaves in late spring — unsightly but rarely fatal; cut off and discard infested leaves (don't compost). In hot, arid spells golden columbines are prone to spider mites and aphids — hose them off or treat as needed. Powdery mildew appears in humid, crowded, or wet-foliage conditions; good airflow and base watering prevent it. The plant is short-lived by nature; succession from self-seed is the best long-term strategy.[1][2]
care.companions: Pairs beautifully with the blue columbine for a gold-and-blue spring show, plus penstemons, native salvias, prairie smoke, and ferny-leaved partners in cottage, rock, and part-shade plantings. Its long bloom and tolerance of dry shade make it useful under high-branched trees.[2]

edible.card: Flowers in tiny amounts (seeds & roots toxic)
edible.summary: Like other columbines, the flowers were historically eaten in tiny amounts as a sweet, nectar-flavored garnish, but the mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides and are genuinely poisonous — this is not a casual edible.[4]
edible.parts: Only the flowers have any food history: several Indigenous North American peoples sucked nectar from columbine blooms or ate small numbers of flowers, and the petals have been used in tiny quantities as a sweet, colorful salad garnish — the sweetness is the nectar held in the long spurs. The seeds, roots, and foliage are not food.[4]
edible.preparation: If used at all, only a few fresh flowers as a garnish — never the seeds, roots, or leaves, and never in quantity. There is no safe way to prepare the seeds or roots for eating. Given documented columbine poisonings, the conservative approach is to treat this plant as ornamental and not eat it.[4]
edible.caution: The mature seeds and roots contain cyanogenic glycosides that are dangerous to the heart if eaten: documented columbine cases include convulsions, respiratory distress, and heart failure after eating fresh foliage, and a child who ate a dozen flowers developed limb weakness, cyanosis, drowsiness, and pinpoint pupils. Keep seeds, roots, and foliage away from children and pets, and do not rely on the old 'flowers are safe' assumption — treat the whole plant with caution.[4][5]

gaps: Photos are species-representative Aquilegia chrysantha (the wild golden columbine), not the named 'Denver Gold' seed selection specifically — no open-licensed cultivar-labeled photo exists. 'Denver Gold' is a Plant Select selection of this species with the same clear-yellow long-spurred flowers, so the species shots match its described look.
